meta.title: 11.2 Stream Processing
meta.description: Kafka Streams, Flink, and real-time processing. Windowing, watermarks, and exactly-once semantics.

blocks.prompt: You need to detect fraudulent transactions within 5 seconds of occurrence. Batch processing runs hourly. What do you need instead?

blocks.content:
## Stream processing

Process unbounded data in real-time as it arrives.

### Key concepts

**Windowing:** Group events into time-based windows for aggregation.
- **Tumbling window:** Fixed, non-overlapping (every 5 minutes)
- **Sliding window:** Overlapping (5-minute window, sliding every 1 minute)
- **Session window:** Gap-based (group events with <30 seconds between them)

**Watermarks:** Handle late-arriving events. A watermark says "all events before time T have arrived." Events arriving after the watermark are either dropped or processed in a correction.

**Exactly-once semantics:** Guarantee each event is processed exactly once, even with failures. Achieved through: idempotent writes, transactional producers (Kafka), and checkpointing (Flink).

### Frameworks

| Framework | Strengths |
|---|---|
| Kafka Streams | Lightweight library (no cluster needed), Java/Kotlin |
| Apache Flink | Full-featured, true streaming, exactly-once, stateful processing |
| Spark Structured Streaming | Micro-batch (not true streaming), familiar Spark API |
| Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics | Managed, serverless |

**Choose Kafka Streams** for simple stream processing that runs within your application. **Choose Flink** for complex event processing, large state, or sub-second latency requirements.

Stream processing handles unbounded real-time data with windowing, watermarks, and exactly-once guarantees. Kafka Streams for simple cases; Flink for complex stateful processing. Windowing strategy depends on your aggregation needs.

blocks.prompt: Module 11.3 covers event sourcing — storing events as the source of truth instead of current state.
